tweets.content:
BREAKING: U.S. Launches Fresh Strikes on Over 80 Targets Across Iran

The U.S. military says it has carried out precision strikes on more than 80 targets in Iran, describing the operation as a response to alleged Iranian attacks on commercial vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz.

According to U.S. Central Command (CENTCOM), the strikes targeted:

-Iranian air defence systems
-Command-and-control facilities
-Coastal radar sites
-Anti-ship missile capabilities
-More than 60 IRGC fast attack boats operating near the Strait of Hormuz

The U.S. says the operation was intended to degrade Iran's ability to threaten international shipping.

Meanwhile, Iranian authorities said no civilian casualties have been reported, although explosions and fires were reported in Bandar Abbas, Sirik, and Qeshm Island.

tweets.title: India's Information Fusion Centre – Indian Ocean Region (IFC-IOR) reports piracy resurges in Western Indian Ocean with 14 incidents since May; Details Indian Navy actions to thwart incidents.
tweets.content:
India's Information Fusion Centre – Indian Ocean Region (IFC-IOR) reports piracy resurges in Western Indian Ocean with 14 incidents since May; Details Indian Navy actions to thwart incidents.

IFC-IOR (@IFC_IOR)

⚠️ #IFCIOR | PIRACY UPDATE 02 – WESTERN IOR | AS ON 1300 UTC 07 JUL 26

Piracy-related activity in the Western Indian Ocean continues to indicate a sustained and adaptive threat pattern. Since 26 May 26, IFC-IOR has recorded 14 piracy-related incidents, comprising 06 attacks and 08 suspicious approaches / attempts, with 02 incidents actioned by Indian Navy.

📌 Assessment of the chronology and spatial distribution of incidents indicates that 02 Pirate Action Groups (PAGs) are likely active, one along the Al Mukalla–Bosaso axis and another operating off Aden. The distribution of incidents suggests that piracy-linked activity is not isolated, but reflects a wider operating pattern across the Gulf of Aden and adjoining approaches.

🌊 Recent incidents also indicate a shift of piracy-linked activity from Somali basin towards the Gulf of Aden. This is likely influenced by prevailing rough sea conditions in the wider Western IOR, which may be pushing skiffs towards relatively sheltered waters and more predictable merchant traffic routes.

🚢 The incident profile shows a clear focus on larger merchant vessels, including tankers, bulk carriers and cargo/container ships. This indicates that PAG activity remains oriented towards high-value commercial shipping rather than small local craft.

🛡️ Indian Navy actions involving MV MASHALLAH 1, MV FAREEDA 5 and MV GOLDEN ARSENAL highlight the operational value of early reporting, rapid IFC-IOR information fusion and coordinated maritime security response. These actions helped compress the response timeline and enabled timely intervention before incidents could escalate further.

📢 Masters, CSOs and operators transiting the affected area are advised to maintain heightened vigilance, implement BMP (MS) measures, keep citadel and emergency response procedures ready, maintain continuous watch, and report all suspicious activity without delay to IFC-IOR and maritime reporting centres.

📧 Report incidents / suspicious activity:
ifc-ior.gurugram@navy.gov.in

@indiannavy
#MaritimeSecurity

And the country actually named in those stories? It just signed with India, President in the room. Markets are the one audience propaganda cannot capture. The minerals. India will build nickel, steel and rare earth magnet manufacturing inside Indonesia, where Chinese firms dominate processing today. Remember Indian automakers panicking within weeks of China’s magnet export curbs? This is the answer. The trust layer. UPI integrates with Indonesia’s payments. IIM Bangalore opens a campus there. India helps build their voting machines. And Indian archaeologists will restore Prambanan. A country lets you touch its elections and its temples only when the trust is total. Now zoom out. tweets.content:
Today India signed a deal to sell supersonic missiles to the world’s largest Muslim-majority country.

In the same set of agreements: India will restore their 1,170-year-old Shiva temple.

If that surprises you, you don’t yet understand how India plays. Let me explain.

Indonesia is India’s largest maritime neighbour. Indira Point, our southernmost tip, sits closer to Aceh than to mainland India.

We don’t share a border. We share a sea.

And we’ve been crossing it for 2,000 years.

Kalinga traders sailed to Java before Christ; Odisha still celebrates Bali Jatra for those voyages. In 1025 CE, Rajendra Chola sent a war fleet across these exact waters.

The Ramayana travelled with the traders. It never left.

Indonesia’s national airline is Garuda. Every full moon at Prambanan, the Ramayana is performed as ballet.

Mostly by Muslim artists.

Indonesians say it themselves: we changed our religion, not our culture.

1947: The Dutch are trying to recolonise Indonesia. Biju Patnaik flies a Dakota into occupied Java and smuggles out their Prime Minister.

Jakarta never forgot. The story goes Sukarno asked Patnaik to name his newborn daughter: Megawati, later President of Indonesia.

And when India held its first ever Republic Day parade in 1950, the chief guest was Sukarno.

1955, Bandung: the same two giants co-wrote non-alignment itself. The instinct: nobody’s junior partner.

Then both drifted for decades.

The last decade rebuilt the bridge. Act East. MAHASAGAR. The 2018 partnership that quietly got India access to Sabang. Prabowo as Republic Day chief guest in 2025, exactly 75 years after Sukarno.

That was the runway. Today was the takeoff.

What got signed:

The port. India and Indonesia will jointly develop Sabang, at the northern mouth of the Malacca Strait, 160 km from India’s own Great Nicobar project.

A fifth of global trade and a third of the world’s seaborne oil passes through that strait. Most of China’s energy too. Beijing has called it the “Malacca Dilemma” for 20 years.

India now sits at both ends of the entrance.

Nobody needs to choke anything. Sitting at the gate is the position.

The missiles. Indonesia becomes the FIRST foreign buyer of India’s Astra missile, and is expanding its BrahMos arsenal.

ASEAN’s largest military, spending its own money, after watching Indian systems perform in May 2025.

India’s defence exports this year: Rs 38,424 crore, up 62% (official MoD numbers).

Meanwhile, our western neighbour spent months planting anonymous stories about JF-17 sales. To Libya (under a UN arms embargo). To Sudan (in a civil war). Even to Indonesia.

And the country actually named in those stories? It just signed with India, President in the room.

Markets are the one audience propaganda cannot capture.

The minerals. India will build nickel, steel and rare earth magnet manufacturing inside Indonesia, where Chinese firms dominate processing today.

Remember Indian automakers panicking within weeks of China’s magnet export curbs?

This is the answer.

The trust layer. UPI integrates with Indonesia’s payments. IIM Bangalore opens a campus there. India helps build their voting machines. And Indian archaeologists will restore Prambanan.

A country lets you touch its elections and its temples only when the trust is total.

Now zoom out.

The world is hardening into two blocs. Every middle power wants a third option: capability without allegiance.

That is India’s entire product. Missiles without bases. Investment without debt traps. Rails without surveillance.

India isn’t building a bloc. It’s building the alternative to blocs.

The two countries that wrote non-alignment in 1955 just wrote its 2026 edition.

A thousand years ago, a Chola fleet crossed these waters and the world took note.

Today India returned. Not with a fleet. With ports, missiles, payment rails and archaeologists.

Empires announce themselves.

Civilisations just resume 🇮🇳 🇮🇩

tweets.title: BANGLADESH APPROVES STARLINK TO EXPORT UNFILTERED INTERNET TO NEIGHBOURING COUNTRIES Bangladesh has approved Starlink to export unfiltered internet bandwidth to neighbouring countries through its telecom infrastructure. Unlike Bangladesh's domestic internet, which passes through government monitoring and filtering systems, the exported traffic will bypass those filters, providing direct international connectivity. Officials say: - The service is only for customers outside Bangladesh - Domestic internet traffic will remain separate and subject to existing regulations - Dedicated infrastructure and monitoring systems will keep domestic and export networks isolated The move marks Bangladesh's first approval for a satellite internet provider to export unfiltered international bandwidth, with Starlink sourcing capacity primarily from the state-owned Bangladesh Submarine Cable Company Limited (BSCCL).
tweets.content:
BANGLADESH APPROVES STARLINK TO EXPORT UNFILTERED INTERNET TO NEIGHBOURING COUNTRIES

Bangladesh has approved Starlink to export unfiltered internet bandwidth to neighbouring countries through its telecom infrastructure.

Unlike Bangladesh's domestic internet, which passes through government monitoring and filtering systems, the exported traffic will bypass those filters, providing direct international connectivity.

Officials say:
- The service is only for customers outside Bangladesh
- Domestic internet traffic will remain separate and subject to existing regulations
- Dedicated infrastructure and monitoring systems will keep domestic and export networks isolated

The move marks Bangladesh's first approval for a satellite internet provider to export unfiltered international bandwidth, with Starlink sourcing capacity primarily from the state-owned Bangladesh Submarine Cable Company Limited (BSCCL).

Key Features of INS Mahendragiri: - Stealth Technology: A special design that makes the ship hard to spot on enemy radar. - Heavy Firepower: Fitted with surface-to-air and surface-to-surface missiles to attack enemy ships, planes, and land targets. - Anti-Submarine Defense: Includes advanced systems to detect and fight underwater threats. - Design: Crafted by the Navy's own Warship Design Bureau. Named after the Mahendragiri mountain range in the Eastern Ghats, the warship was designed by the Indian Navy’s Warship Design Bureau (WDB) and built by Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders Limited (MDL) in Mumbai.
tweets.content:
INDIAN NAVY TO COMMISSION STEALTH FRIGATE INS MAHENDRAGIRI

The Indian Navy is set to commission INS Mahendragiri (F38), its sixth indigenous Project 17A stealth frigate, at Visakhapatnam on 11 July 2026.

Key Features of INS Mahendragiri:

- Stealth Technology: A special design that makes the ship hard to spot on enemy radar.
- Heavy Firepower: Fitted with surface-to-air and surface-to-surface missiles to attack enemy ships, planes, and land targets.
- Anti-Submarine Defense: Includes advanced systems to detect and fight underwater threats.
- Design: Crafted by the Navy's own Warship Design Bureau.

Named after the Mahendragiri mountain range in the Eastern Ghats, the warship was designed by the Indian Navy’s Warship Design Bureau (WDB) and built by Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders Limited (MDL) in Mumbai.

tweets.title: #Exclusive : 🇮🇳 IHQ MoD(Navy) has initiated a process to develop Containerised Theatre Level Sonar (CTLS) and the doctrine behind it is the real story. This isn’t a hull-mounted sonar for a new ASW frigate. It’s a containerised VDS + twin thin-line array package, deployable on ANY suitable IN ship/auxiliary the only mandatory ship-fit is a certified A-frame + winch. Everything else plugs in. Combined with mono/bi/multi-static operating modes, this points to India building toward a networked, ship-agnostic ASW sensor grid Think CAPTAS/Nixie-style VDS logic, but boxed for rapid deployment on OPVs and auxiliaries rather than dedicated ASW combatants. Why it matters: with frigate/corvette production timelines stretched, this lets IN turn any hull with deck space into an ad-hoc ASW node A force-multiplier that decouples ASW coverage from new hull construction.
